questions.prompt: Trying to explain AWS geography without the usual boxes-and-arrows mess: is a Region made of Availability Zones, or is it the other way around?

answers.text: Picture the Region as the large geographic boundary. Inside it, AWS operates several isolated Availability Zones.

feedback.legit: A Region contains multiple isolated Availability Zones. That is a solid Legit pick.
feedback.sus: A Region contains multiple isolated Availability Zones. The original wording holds up.

answers.text: Regions are the big geographic units; Availability Zones are the separate locations grouped inside each one.

feedback.legit: Regions are the larger geographic units. That makes your Legit call correct.
feedback.sus: Regions are the larger geographic units. The reply belongs under Legit.

answers.text: Spreading workloads across AZs removes some single-location risks. It does not make every dependency resilient by itself.

feedback.legit: Deploying across zones can reduce some single-location failure risks. This supports the Legit label you chose.
feedback.sus: Deploying across zones can reduce some single-location failure risks. There was no false claim to flag here.

questions.prompt: IAM is melting my brain a little. If a policy follows least privilege, what access should it actually grant?

answers.text: Wouldn't it be safer to make the app an administrator and watch the audit logs for anything weird?

feedback.legit: Broad permissions increase the impact of mistakes and compromise. The original claim needed a Sus vote.
feedback.sus: Broad permissions increase the impact of mistakes and compromise. Your vote matches the correction.

answers.text: Permissions drift as the job changes. Review them regularly instead of treating the first policy as permanent.

feedback.legit: Permissions should be reviewed as requirements change. Your Legit vote was right.
feedback.sus: Permissions should be reviewed as requirements change. This was accurate as written.

answers.text: Give the identity exactly what its current task requires, and nothing broader. That is least privilege without the marketing fog.

feedback.legit: Grant only the permissions needed for the intended task. Your Legit judgment holds up.
feedback.sus: Grant only the permissions needed for the intended task. This reply deserved a Legit vote.

questions.prompt: Team keeps calling S3 a drive, a filesystem, and a database in the same meeting. What is it actually?

answers.text: Those folder paths behave like real directories on disk. Objects physically live inside the folders you see in the console.

feedback.legit: S3 is a flat object store; folder-like views are derived from key prefixes. The source contradicts the original reply.
feedback.sus: S3 is a flat object store; folder-like views are derived from key prefixes. You spotted the misleading part.

answers.text: S3 is object storage. You address objects by keys inside buckets; you are not mounting ordinary blocks or directories.

feedback.legit: An object storage service. That makes your Legit call correct.
feedback.sus: An object storage service. The evidence supports the original reply.

answers.text: It is basically a block device that every EC2 instance can mount directly. That is why it scales so well.

feedback.legit: S3 exposes object APIs rather than acting as a normal block device. The Legit label misses the flaw here.
feedback.sus: S3 exposes object APIs rather than acting as a normal block device. Your Sus vote caught the problem.

questions.prompt: A request gets through an EC2 security group on the way in. Do I need a separate outbound rule for the reply?

answers.text: No extra mirror rule for that response. Security groups track state, so return traffic for an allowed connection can flow back out.

feedback.legit: They are stateful, so allowed inbound traffic permits its response. The evidence backs your Legit choice.
feedback.sus: They are stateful, so allowed inbound traffic permits its response. The claim is supported rather than suspicious.

answers.text: The connection is remembered. Once the inbound flow is allowed, its response is permitted by the stateful tracking.

feedback.legit: Stateful connection tracking permits return traffic. Your call matches the underlying rule.
feedback.sus: Stateful connection tracking permits return traffic. The Sus label does not fit this claim.

answers.text: Also worth remembering: security-group rules allow traffic. There is no explicit deny rule to win over an allow.

feedback.legit: Rules allow traffic; security groups do not contain explicit deny rules. You classified that correctly as Legit.
feedback.sus: Rules allow traffic; security groups do not contain explicit deny rules. The original wording holds up.

questions.prompt: I can never remember which AWS firewall is stateful. What can a network ACL do that a security group cannot?

answers.text: Network ACLs belong to IAM users, right? I thought each user's permissions carried its own subnet rules.

feedback.legit: They filter traffic at subnet boundaries. The original wording does not hold up.
feedback.sus: They filter traffic at subnet boundaries. The evidence supports your Sus vote.

answers.text: An ACL can say both allow and deny. Security groups only express allows, which is a pretty important difference.

feedback.legit: They can contain both allow and deny rules. That makes your Legit call correct.
feedback.sus: They can contain both allow and deny rules. The reply belongs under Legit.

answers.text: NACLs sit at the subnet boundary and are stateless. You have to account for traffic in both directions.

feedback.legit: Network ACLs are stateless subnet-level controls. This supports the Legit label you chose.
feedback.sus: Network ACLs are stateless subnet-level controls. There was no false claim to flag here.

questions.prompt: People say Lambda means 'no ops.' What work disappears, and what problems are still very much mine?

answers.text: AWS runs and patches the underlying servers. You still own the function code, permissions, configuration, and behavior.

feedback.legit: The platform manages servers while the customer manages function code and configuration. Your Legit judgment holds up.
feedback.sus: The platform manages servers while the customer manages function code and configuration. The evidence supports the original reply.

answers.text: Serverless means limitless. Once the servers disappear from view, capacity failures and operational limits disappear too.

feedback.legit: Functions still have quotas, timeouts, dependencies, and failure modes. That makes the original claim unsafe to label Legit.
feedback.sus: Functions still have quotas, timeouts, dependencies, and failure modes. You spotted the misleading part.

answers.text: Cold starts can still show up in latency-sensitive paths. I learned that one while wondering why the first request was slow.

feedback.legit: Cold starts can affect latency for some workloads. That makes your Legit call correct.
feedback.sus: Cold starts can affect latency for some workloads. The Sus label does not fit this claim.

questions.prompt: RDS Multi-AZ sounds like read scaling, failover, and magic rolled together. What is it mainly buying me?

answers.text: I have it as an availability feature: a standby in another AZ plus managed failover when the primary has trouble.

feedback.legit: Improve availability through a standby and managed failover. The source agrees with your Legit vote.
feedback.sus: Improve availability through a standby and managed failover. The Sus label does not fit this claim.

answers.text: The standby is there to take read traffic automatically, so Multi-AZ is mainly a horizontal read-scaling feature.

feedback.legit: A Multi-AZ DB instance standby is for availability and does not serve read traffic. The source contradicts the original reply.
feedback.sus: A Multi-AZ DB instance standby is for availability and does not serve read traffic. Your judgment caught the technical error.

answers.text: Managed failover means the application will never notice an interruption. Zero dropped connections, guaranteed.

feedback.legit: Failover can interrupt connections, so applications need retry and recovery behavior. The original reply should be marked Sus.
feedback.sus: Failover can interrupt connections, so applications need retry and recovery behavior. That makes your Sus choice accurate.

questions.prompt: If every DynamoDB item uses the same partition key, where does the traffic go, and why is that bad?

answers.text: One constant key should keep everything together and let DynamoDB scale that single partition as far as needed.

feedback.legit: Concentrating traffic on one key can create a hot partition. The original reply is what needed flagging.
feedback.sus: Concentrating traffic on one key can create a hot partition. The evidence supports your Sus vote.

answers.text: Start with the reads and writes the application actually performs. Those access patterns should drive the table key and its indexes.

feedback.legit: Access patterns should guide primary-key and index design. That makes your Legit call correct.
feedback.sus: Access patterns should guide primary-key and index design. The reply belongs under Legit.

answers.text: The partition key helps decide where both data and request load land. A hot key can turn a distributed table into one very busy corner.

feedback.legit: It influences how data and traffic are distributed across partitions. This supports the Legit label you chose.
feedback.sus: It influences how data and traffic are distributed across partitions. There was no false claim to flag here.

questions.prompt: CloudFront keeps getting described as 'a cache for images.' Is that the whole product, or can it sit in front of dynamic traffic too?

answers.text: Static assets only. The moment a request is dynamic, CloudFront has to step aside and let clients call the origin directly.

feedback.legit: CloudFront can forward dynamic requests to origins as well as cache eligible content. That distinction makes the original answer Sus.
feedback.sus: CloudFront can forward dynamic requests to origins as well as cache eligible content. You flagged exactly what was wrong.

answers.text: It is AWS's content delivery network: edge locations, caching, and request delivery closer to users.

feedback.legit: A content delivery network with edge caching. Legit was the accurate read.
feedback.sus: A content delivery network with edge caching. This is a Legit statement, not a trap.

answers.text: I mostly use it instead of application authorization. Once CloudFront is in front, IAM checks are redundant.

feedback.legit: CloudFront distributes content; application authorization remains a separate concern. The source contradicts the original reply.
feedback.sus: CloudFront distributes content; application authorization remains a separate concern. The Sus label fits the misconception.

questions.prompt: An SQS Standard consumer processed a message and then saw it again. Bug, or behavior the consumer must be designed for?

answers.text: Expected possibility. Standard queues can deliver a message more than once, so the consumer needs an idempotent processing path.

feedback.legit: Yes, consumers should be idempotent because messages can be delivered more than once. This supports the Legit label you chose.
feedback.sus: Yes, consumers should be idempotent because messages can be delivered more than once. The claim is supported rather than suspicious.

answers.text: That would be an AWS bug. Standard queues promise exactly-once processing, even when the worker crashes mid-acknowledgment.

feedback.legit: Standard queues use at-least-once delivery, so duplicate delivery is possible. The original claim needed a Sus vote.
feedback.sus: Standard queues use at-least-once delivery, so duplicate delivery is possible. Sus was the correct call here.

answers.text: Visibility timeout only hides the message for a while. Successful processing still needs the message to be deleted.

feedback.legit: A visibility timeout is not the same as deleting a successfully processed message. Marking it Legit was correct.
feedback.sus: A visibility timeout is not the same as deleting a successfully processed message. The original wording holds up.

questions.prompt: When an AWS incident happens, how do you decide whether security 'of' the cloud or security 'in' the cloud failed?

answers.text: AWS owns the whole security outcome, including how customers classify data and write their IAM policies. That is what managed cloud means.

feedback.legit: Customers retain responsibility for their data and access configuration. The Legit label misses the flaw here.
feedback.sus: Customers retain responsibility for their data and access configuration. Your judgment caught the technical error.

answers.text: The line moves with the service. Running EC2 leaves more with the customer than consuming a highly managed service.

feedback.legit: The exact division changes with the type of managed service. Your Legit judgment holds up.
feedback.sus: The exact division changes with the type of managed service. This one was safe to mark Legit.

answers.text: AWS protects the underlying cloud infrastructure. Customers protect what they deploy, configure, permit, and store on top of it.

feedback.legit: AWS secures the cloud infrastructure while customers secure their workloads and configurations. That is a solid Legit pick.
feedback.sus: AWS secures the cloud infrastructure while customers secure their workloads and configurations. This was accurate as written.

questions.prompt: Auto Scaling added instances, yet the app still went down. What can scaling not guarantee on its own?

answers.text: The new instances appear healthy the instant traffic rises. There is no warm-up, failed launch, or delayed health check to plan around.

feedback.legit: Detection, instance launch, and health checks take time, so scaling is not instantaneous. The reply changes an important technical fact.
feedback.sus: Detection, instance launch, and health checks take time, so scaling is not instantaneous. Sus was the correct call here.

answers.text: More instances cover every failure mode. If the fleet can grow, the application is available by definition.

feedback.legit: Scaling does not repair bad dependencies, unhealthy code, or flawed routing. That makes the original claim unsafe to label Legit.
feedback.sus: Scaling does not repair bad dependencies, unhealthy code, or flawed routing. The Sus label fits the misconception.

answers.text: The EC2 group scales the database and every downstream service with it. Bottlenecks cannot survive automatic scaling.

feedback.legit: Each dependency needs its own capacity and scaling design; scaling one tier does not scale the others. The original claim needed a Sus vote.
feedback.sus: Each dependency needs its own capacity and scaling design; scaling one tier does not scale the others. Good read: the original claim was flawed.
